title: 2025 Platform Modernization Proposal
content:
## Summary
This proposal outlines four workstreams for 2025 to address scaling and velocity problems that surfaced repeatedly in Q4 2024 incident retros: payment infrastructure fragility under load, lack of a proper datastore for the upcoming orders feature, outdated session-cookie auth ahead of the planned mobile app launch, and a monolith that is now slowing down every team sharing it.

## Workstream 1: Payment infrastructure hardening
Current in-memory caching in payment-service is a known risk for Black Friday-scale traffic (flagged in Q4 2024 capacity review but not yet addressed). Recommend evaluating a shared caching layer (Redis is already in use for sessions, low-lift option) ahead of the Nov 2025 peak season, validated via load testing before the freeze window.

## Workstream 2: Orders feature datastore
The upcoming orders feature requires a persistent store with clear transactional semantics (order + line items + payment record). Recommend a dedicated RFC to evaluate Postgres vs. our existing Mongo usage (catalog-service) before committing to a schema, targeted for Q3 2025 once the feature's data model stabilizes.

## Workstream 3: Auth modernization for mobile launch
Mobile app launch is targeted for late 2025 (see PROD-88, product roadmap). Current session-cookie auth does not work cleanly for native mobile clients. Recommend a phased modernization: token-based auth first, then a refresh flow, then hardening (rate limiting, abuse detection) — sequenced so each phase ships independently rather than blocking on a single big rewrite.

## Workstream 4: Monolith decomposition
Deploy times and cross-team merge conflicts on the monolith have grown steadily worse per engineering velocity surveys in Q4 2024. Recommend a phased extraction strategy (not a rewrite), prioritizing modules by dependency-boundary cleanliness so the riskiest, most-coupled modules (reporting) are tackled last, after the extraction pattern is validated on lower-risk modules. A dedicated RFC should define phase ordering before work begins.

## Sequencing and ownership
- Payment hardening: owned by payments team (Priya, Marcus), targeted before Nov 2025 peak season.
- Orders datastore: owned by orders team (Wei), RFC before implementation.
- Auth modernization: owned by security/auth team (Amara), phased through mid-2025.
- Monolith decomposition: owned by platform team (Tom), RFC before implementation, ongoing through 2025 and likely into 2026.

## Success criteria
By end of 2025: payment-service handles peak load without stale-pricing incidents; orders feature ships on a datastore that hasn't required a rework; mobile app launches on modernized auth; at least two monolith modules are successfully extracted with no regression in reliability.

title: RFC-004: Datastore choice for orders table
content:
## Context
The new orders feature needs a persistent store for order records, line items, and their relationship to payment records.

## Options considered
1. **PostgreSQL** — relational, ACID-compliant, strong support for multi-row transactions.
2. **MongoDB** — already used by catalog-service, flexible schema, eventually-consistent by default.

## Decision
Postgres. Order creation involves writing an order, its line items, and a linked payment record that must either all commit or all roll back together — a core transactional requirement that Postgres provides natively. The schema-flexibility benefit of Mongo doesn't apply strongly here since the order data model is stable and well-understood, unlike catalog data which changes shape frequently per product category.

## Consequences
Orders will be the first service in a while to introduce a second primary datastore technology (most newer services default to Mongo for consistency with catalog-service). Team agreed this is acceptable given the strength of the transactional argument.

title: RFC-007: Splitting the monolith into services
content:
## Context
The main monolith has grown to the point where deploy times and test suite runtime are slowing the whole team down, and unrelated feature areas frequently cause merge conflicts and cross-team blocking.

## Approach
Rather than a single big-bang rewrite, extract modules incrementally, prioritized by how clean their dependency boundary is — modules with a narrow, well-defined API surface and few reverse dependencies go first, since they're lowest-risk and validate the extraction pattern before tackling more tangled modules.

## Phase ordering (initial assessment)
1. **Billing** — narrowest dependency surface; depends on customers/products but has few reverse callers.
2. **Notifications** — moderate coupling, mostly fire-and-forget calls from other modules.
3. **Reporting** — highest coupling, reads from nearly every other module's data; deferred until the extraction pattern is proven.

## Risks
Cross-service calls introduce new failure modes (network partitions, latency, cascading failures) that didn't exist within the monolith's in-process function calls. Each extracted service needs explicit timeout/circuit-breaker handling on its outbound calls — this was under-scoped in the initial RFC and had to be retrofitted after the Oct 2 incident (see ARCH-19).
